cataloging_subject.label: Life of Saint Mary Magdalene (Gilte Legende, Durham fragment)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the surviving incomplete Middle English prose Life of Saint Mary Magdalene copied in Durham University Library Cosin MS V.ii.14, ff.106r–111v: a chapter of the anonymous c.1438 Gilte Legende translation from Jean de Vignay's French Légende dorée. It begins with its manuscript rubric and breaks in the final burial sentence because the following leaf is missing. The subject excludes the codex as a physical object, Julius Zupitza's 1893 article and German commentary, Theodor Grässe's Latin comparison text, the University of Michigan encoding, and the checked-in XML; those remain separately modeled lineage layers.

catalog_summary.title: Life of Saint Mary Magdalene (Gilte Legende, Durham fragment)
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator
catalog_summary.date: Translation c.1438; surviving witness mid-fifteenth century; first known publication of this witness 1893
catalog_summary.region: England; exact translation place unknown; Zupitza describes the Durham text's language as East Midland

titles.value: Life of Saint Mary Magdalene (Gilte Legende, Durham fragment)

assertion.notes: The qualifier distinguishes this incomplete Durham witness from the full Gilte Legende chapter and other medieval Mary Magdalene lives.

titles.value: Here bigynnyth the lyfe of Marye Magdaleyn
notes: The red rubric appears mid-page on f.106r after the preceding Life of Saint Margaret; terminal punctuation is editorial.

titles.value: Gilte Legende: Life of Saint Mary Magdalene
Das Leben der heiligen Maria Magdalena

scope.description: Short form of Zupitza's editorial article title

notes: This is a modern German editorial title, not a medieval title or evidence that the work is German.

titles.value: Mary Magdaleyn was surnamed of Magdalon, the castell, and she was born of the kynred, that were descendid of ryal kynne
And seynt Maxymien anoyntid the bodi of hir with

assertion.notes: This is the final surviving phrase, not the work's original complete explicit.

notes: The manuscript and edition break after with; the following leaf of quire 14 is missing.

agents.name: Julius Zupitza
notes: Editor and direct transcriber of the Durham witness, not its medieval author or translator.

assertion.notes: The target is part of the anonymous Gilte Legende translation from Jean de Vignay's French version.

notes: Jacobus de Voragine is the author/compiler of the Latin source work and Jean de Vignay the French translator; neither is silently assigned direct authorship of this Middle English version.
This responsibility describes the 1893 edition represented by the CME item, not medieval intellectual authorship.

scope.description: German textual introduction and the English/Latin comparison apparatus surrounding the edited fragment

notes: The German introduction itself is omitted from CME00108, while comparison markers and footnotes remain in the encoding.

date_statements.display: About 1438, as part of the anonymous Gilte Legende translation

assertion.notes: The circa-1438 date belongs to the Gilte Legende translation project, not to the production of Cosin MS V.ii.14.

date_statements.display: Mid-fifteenth century in Durham University Library Cosin MS V.ii.14

assertion.notes: The current Durham catalog dates the codex to the mid-fifteenth century; Zupitza proposed approximately its third quarter.

date_statements.display: 1893 (first known publication of the Durham witness, by Julius Zupitza)

assertion.notes: The publication date and witness identity are direct; 'first' is qualified because the inspected sources do not provide an exhaustive census of all earlier notices or excerpts.

places.label: East Midlands, England
notes: Used only for Zupitza's linguistic characterization of the Durham text, not as proof of the physical place where the translation or manuscript was produced.

assertion.notes: The work is an English-language translation in an English manuscript; the exact place of translation remains unknown.
Zupitza says the language has a thoroughly East Midland character. This historical claim has not been silently converted into a place-of-composition or modern dialect-profile assertion.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: Latin in CME00108 is Grässe's editorial comparison text; German occurs in Zupitza's notes; French is the source-work language. None makes the cataloged Middle English fragment intrinsically multilingual.

form_statements.description: Continuous hagiographic prose, represented as one paragraph in the English XML stream and subdivided only by Zupitza's 107 comparison numbers.

assertion.notes: The immediate source is Jean de Vignay's French version; Jacobus's Latin work is upstream.

summaries.value: The legend recounts Mary Magdalene's noble family, worldly life, repentance, intimacy with Christ, and place at the Resurrection. After the disciples' dispersion she travels with Lazarus, Martha, Maximinus, and others to Marseilles, preaches against idolatry, and converts a provincial ruler and his wife. Their pilgrimage to Saint Peter includes the wife's death in childbirth, miraculous preservation of mother and child, and restoration on the return voyage. Mary then converts communities, withdraws for thirty years into a desert retreat where angels raise and feed her, receives the Eucharist, and dies. The Durham copy breaks as Maximinus begins to anoint her body.

The surviving fragment runs continuously from the rubric and ancestry of Mary Magdalene through comparison unit 107. It includes the Gospel-derived penitent narrative, the legendary mission to Provence, the ruler's pilgrimage and family miracle, Mary's desert asceticism and angelic elevation, her final communion, and the beginning of her burial. A missing leaf after f.111 prevents completion of the burial and any remaining chapter ending.

The record treats the Middle English fragment as one work unit within the wider Gilte Legende rather than cataloging the whole bilingual 1893 article as a medieval work. Grässe's Latin is a comparison source selected by Zupitza, German is an editorial language, and Jean de Vignay's French and Jacobus's Latin are related source works. The manuscript, edition, digital encoding, and repository artifact are linked but not conflated.

extent.description: One incomplete Middle English prose saint's life on manuscript ff.106r–111v and printed pp.210–224. The English XML stream has one P element, one HEAD, 118 milestones, 22 editorial notes, 191 italic spans, and 14 descendant page breaks plus the opening p.210 page break outside DIV2. Zupitza's 107 comparison numbers are editorial alignment units, not an intrinsic medieval chapter count; the separate Latin P and its 114 milestones are source-edition apparatus rather than work extent.

content_parts.label: Surviving Durham fragment of the Life of Saint Mary Magdalene
content_parts.summary: The continuous surviving chapter from its rubric and opening genealogy through Mary's final communion and death to the incomplete beginning of Maximinus's burial preparations.
notes: The fragment starts mid-page on f.106r after the Life of Saint Margaret but at the beginning of its own text.
It is codicologically incomplete at the end: quire 14 wants the leaf following f.111.
The current Durham HTML's compressed f.106r–v component display is not used as the endpoint because its own explicit, Zupitza, and the digitized images establish continuation through f.111v.

related_works.title: The Gilte Legende
notes: The cataloged unit is one prose chapter/version within the larger anonymous Middle English translation, conventionally dated c.1438.

related_works.title: Jean de Vignay's Légende dorée: Vie de sainte Marie Madeleine
notes: This is the immediate source work identified by current Durham cataloging; the English translator's physical French exemplar remains unknown.

related_works.title: Jacobus de Voragine, Legenda aurea: De sancta Maria Magdalena
notes: The Latin chapter is the ultimate source through Jean de Vignay's French translation. Grässe's 1890 edition is a modern edition selected by Zupitza for comparison, not the medieval translator's physical Latin source.

evidence.locator: IDG; first DIV2 LANG=enm; heading, incipit, final phrase, and complete English stream
evidence.summary: Primary local evidence for work identity, Middle English language, prose form, manuscript rubric, surviving text, content, subjects, edition markers, and the distinction between the English work stream and the Latin comparison stream.

evidence.locator: Element counts split by DIV2 language
evidence.summary: Confirms one English prose paragraph with one heading, 118 milestones, 22 notes, 191 italic spans, and 14 descendant page breaks plus the opening p.210 break; also confirms that the separate Latin stream contains one paragraph, 114 milestones, and 15 page breaks.

evidence.citation: Durham University Library Archives and Special Collections, Catalogue of Cosin MS V.ii.14.
evidence.locator: Manuscript introduction, physical description, item 5, and bibliography
evidence.summary: Current official evidence for the manuscript's identity, mid-fifteenth-century English production, missing leaf after f.111, target title, incipit, incomplete explicit, Middle English language, source in Jean de Vignay's French version of Jacobus, Zupitza edition, and modern Gilte Legende association.
notes: Its HTML component-range display is compressed; the endpoint is established by the linked edition and full facsimile.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence from Julius Zupitza, 'Das Leben der heiligen Maria Magdalena' (1893).
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for the article title and date, direct transcription from legacy shelfmark 5.2.14 at ff.106r–111v, incompleteness, proposed French intermediary, Grässe comparison, East Midland linguistic characterization, capitalization normalization, comparison units, and surviving text.

evidence.citation: Linked visual inspection of Durham's complete digital facsimile of Cosin MS V.ii.14.
evidence.summary: Confirms the work's mid-page start, red rubric and decorated initial, twelve-canvas extent, incomplete final phrase on f.111v, and representative correspondence between italicized edition segments and manuscript abbreviation signs.

evidence.citation: University of St Andrews, Middle English Texts Series, 'Three Lives from the Gilte Legende.'
evidence.summary: Supports the anonymous Gilte Legende's identity as a major prose translation of about 1438 and the modern Hamer/Russell EETS edition.

evidence.citation: Folger Shakespeare Library catalog record 197393, Gilte Legende.
evidence.summary: Supports the Middle English translation's immediate dependence on Jean de Vignay's French Légende dorée and the French work's dependence on Jacobus's Latin Legenda aurea.

evidence.citation: Bibliothèque nationale de France, Catalogue général record FRBNF31606933, Légende dorée / Jacques de Voragine; traduction de Jean de Vignay.
evidence.summary: Authority evidence distinguishing Jacobus de Voragine as author of the Latin source work from Jean de Vignay as translator of the French version.

evidence.citation: Linked CME00108 lineage manifest access and bibliographic records.
evidence.summary: Supports the U-M title display, Zupitza editor responsibility, stable digital item, and exact public access paths without treating the U-M record's editor as the medieval author.

open_questions.question: Which French manuscript or textual family of Jean de Vignay's Légende dorée supplied the exemplar for the c.1438 English Gilte Legende translation represented here?
next_steps: Consult Hamer and Russell's full EETS Gilte Legende edition and its source study.
Compare the Durham chapter's distinctive additions, omissions, and errors against critical work on Jean de Vignay.
Do not promote a merely representative surviving French manuscript to direct-source status without textual evidence.

open_questions.question: Can the anonymous Gilte Legende translator or the exact place of this chapter's translation be identified more narrowly than England, and does modern dialect analysis confirm Zupitza's East Midland characterization of the Durham text?
next_steps: Consult the EETS edition's linguistic introduction and current scholarship on Gilte Legende manuscripts.
Distinguish translator language, Durham scribal language, and place of manuscript production before adding a composition-place facet.
Retain East Midlands as a historical dialect claim with medium confidence until reassessed.

open_questions.question: Can all 191 italic spans in the English stream be assigned precise editorial functions from the manuscript images?
next_steps: Audit every span against ff.106r–111v at the best resolution Durham permits.
Separate abbreviation expansions, supplied letters, uncertain readings, and italic German commentary.
Preserve the edition's typography until segment-level evidence supports more precise markup.

notes: The preferred title is supplied for discovery; the manuscript's own red rubric is separately preserved as an original title statement.
Anonymous Middle English translator is the authorship display. Julius Zupitza is editor and transcriber, not medieval author; Jean de Vignay and Jacobus de Voragine belong to source-work layers.
The c.1438 date describes the Gilte Legende translation, the mid-fifteenth-century date describes the Durham witness, and 1893 describes Zupitza's publication. These dates are not interchangeable.
The East Midland label is Zupitza's linguistic characterization, not a demonstrated place of translation or manuscript production.
Latin, German, and French are deliberately excluded from the work-level language facet: they belong respectively to Zupitza's comparison source, editorial commentary, and the upstream source work.
The missing final leaf makes the cataloged Durham text a fragment even though the underlying Gilte Legende chapter was originally complete.
The full Durham facsimile is cached privately and Git-ignored; the metadata points from the full codex to ff.106r–111v rather than caching a work-only crop.
